risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization was generated by a third party physician using tables of random numbers." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random allocation method (tables of random numbers) and indicates the sequence was generated by a third party, which supports truly random sequence generation.

_evidence.method_quote: Randomization was generated by a third party physician using tables of random numbers.
_evidence.method_summary: A third-party physician generated the allocation sequence using tables of random numbers.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization was generated by a third party physician using tables of random numbers. Care providers and physician assessing outcomes were blinded for each other works and results." Comment: This describes random sequence generation and some blinding, but it does not state any method for concealing the allocation sequence from the person enrolling participants. No explicit central allocation, sealed opaque sequential envelopes, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is reported, so allocation concealment remains unclear.

_evidence.note: The article describes randomization as being generated by a third party physician using tables of random numbers, but gives no information about concealment of the allocation sequence.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This study is a randomized, double-blind, controlled clinical trial with four active medication conditions" and "Care providers and physician assessing outcomes were blinded for each other works and results." Comment: The study explicitly states that it was double-blind and that care providers were blinded, indicating participants/personnel blinding was intended and implemented. Because the main outcomes included subjective clinical response and side effects, successful blinding would be important, and the report provides positive evidence of blinding rather than absence of blinding.

_evidence.blinding_quote: This study is a randomized, double-blind, controlled clinical trial with four active medication conditions
_evidence.blinding_method: double-blind controlled clinical trial; care providers and physician assessing outcomes were blinded for each other works and results

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Care providers and physician assessing outcomes were blinded for each other works and results." Comment: The study explicitly states that the physician who assessed outcomes was blinded. The main efficacy outcome (PANSS positive subscales / time to first response) and the reported side-effect outcomes were assessed by this blinded physician, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence measurement. Although some outcomes are subjective/judgement-dependent, the assessor blinding makes detection bias unlikely.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Care providers and physician assessing outcomes were blinded for each other works and results.

outcomes.label: PANSS positive subscales (P1-P6) / time to first response
drug side-effects / EPS (Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale-National Institute of Mental Health scale)
sedation, blurry vision, dry mouth, constipation, urine retention, rash
complete blood count, electrocardiography, AST, ALT, fasting blood sugar, blood pressure, body weight, body mass index

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A total of 68 patients screened and 41 met all inclusion and no exclusion criteria, which allocated randomly into the four intervention groups" ... "Clinical response was observed in 97.5% (N = 39) of subjects within 2-weeks study and only one subject (in olanzapine group) did not respond after 14 days." Comment: The analysed outcome at the 2-week time point appears to have complete follow-up for 40/40 participants in the four randomized arms shown in Table 1 (10 per arm analysed, 0 lost in each arm in the extracted CONSORT flow), with only one nonresponse rather than a loss to follow-up. There is no reported post-randomization dropout or exclusion affecting the analysed outcome, so attrition is essentially absent and unlikely to bias the result.

per_arm_flow.reasons: Clinical response was observed in 97.5% (N = 39) of subjects within 2-weeks study and only one subject (in olanzapine group) did not respond after 14 days.

_evidence.notes: Table 1 caption reports n=40, while Results say 41 were randomized into four groups. One subject (olanzapine group) did not respond after 14 days; no separate dropout/loss-to-follow-up was described, and the analyses reported were based on observed outcomes (e.g., paired t-test, survival analysis).
